facts.fact: SCD Type 2 preserves full audit trail by inserting new rows on attribute changes instead of updating existing rows, with surrogate keys decoupling queries from business attribute mutations.

SCD Type 2 requires five additional columns: surrogate_key (immutable identifier), effective_start_date, effective_end_date, is_current (boolean flag), and source_natural_key (for tracing upstream changes).

Expand-contract pattern reduces schema migration downtime to zero by running three phases: (1) expand adds new columns/tables alongside old ones, (2) migrate updates application to use both schemas in parallel, (3) contract removes deprecated structures after migration completes.
pgroll (Xata's open-source tool for PostgreSQL) automates expand-contract pattern by serving multiple schema versions simultaneously, enabling reversible migrations with zero locking.

Bridge tables (junction tables) in M:N relationships reduce storage by consolidating multiple foreign key columns into a single column with low cardinality, improving compression and query performance.

Surrogate keys (auto-incremented integers) do not change while a row exists, whereas natural keys risk cascading updates across fact tables if upstream business attributes mutate (e.g., email address change invalidates key).

In data warehouses, natural keys as primary keys create coupling to upstream systems; migrating to a new system requires full schema refactor. Surrogate keys decouple analytics from operational system changes.

Low-cardinality columns (Boolean, category flags) on B-tree indexes force sequential table scans because the index returns many row pointers per key value, making full-table scan faster than index lookup.
Cardinality estimation in query optimizers uses column statistics histograms to predict result set sizes; misestimates cause join operation order changes and 10-100x query slowdowns.
Star schema fact tables with dimension foreign keys enable 5-50x faster aggregations than normalized schemas because denormalization eliminates expensive multi-table joins.

Aggregate fact tables (pre-computed summaries at higher hierarchy levels) deliver 10-100x speedup for dashboard queries by eliminating need to scan granular fact tables and compute GROUP BY operations.
Denormalization in OLAP workloads (data warehouses) trades write performance for read performance; one education firm reduced query time from 2 min 13 sec to 8 sec on 31M rows via denormalization.

Foreign key cascading actions (ON UPDATE CASCADE, ON DELETE CASCADE) automate referential integrity but can silently delete large subsets of data in child tables if parent key is updated; requires careful testing and backups.

Bounded contexts in domain-driven design define entity boundaries by human culture (where language changes), not by database schema; a 'Customer' in billing bounded context differs semantically from 'Customer' in shipping context.
PostgreSQL CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY builds indexes without full-table locks, enabling large-table index creation in production; mutex contention still queues non-concurrent DDL.

pt-online-schema-change (Percona Toolkit for MySQL) executes DDL by creating shadow table, copying data in batches, swapping tables atomically, and dropping old table—all with row-level locks instead of full-table lock.
Regulatory compliance (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR) explicitly mandates complete audit trails via SCD Type 2; financial institutions rely on full version history to prove data integrity during audits.

Stripe data refreshes every 3 hours with full load snapshots into Snowflake/Redshift/Databricks; fact tables record transaction events with foreign keys to dimension tables (charge, customer, invoice).
Reshape (Postgres zero-downtime migration tool) automatically handles complex migrations (e.g., column type changes, multi-step backfills) that would normally require manual multi-step deployment.

SCD Type 2 audit trails enable historical point-in-time reporting—e.g., 'what was the product price on 2024-06-15?'—by filtering on effective_start_date and effective_end_date without full-table rewrites.

examples.scenario: E-commerce platform tracks product pricing changes (SCD Type 2)
examples.description: When product price changes from $50 to $45, the system inserts a new row with new is_current=true, sets old row is_current=false and effective_end_date=now(). Historical reports correctly show $50 price for orders before change, $45 after.

examples.scenario: User address mutates; system uses surrogate key strategy
examples.description: User record has surrogate user_id=12345 (immutable). Mailing address moves from New York to California. New address row inserted with user_id=12345 (foreign key unchanged), old address marked inactive. Fact tables referencing user_id=12345 remain valid without updates.
examples.metric: Zero cascading updates across 50M+ fact table rows

examples.scenario: Netflix movie-to-genre M:N relationship scaled with bridge table
examples.description: Instead of storing all genres in a genres array column (causing full-table scan on genre filters), Netflix uses: (movie_id, genre_id) bridge table. Queries filter via bridge table join (fast cardinality 2), not array contains operator.
examples.real_company: Netflix (inferred from standard data warehouse architecture)
examples.metric: 50x faster genre-filtered searches via index on (genre_id, movie_id)

examples.scenario: Denormalization in education platform reduced query time
examples.description: Dashboard query originally scanned 8 tables with 7 joins to compute 'student completion rate by module'. Denormalized into aggregate_student_completion table (pre-computed daily). Query time: 2 min 13 sec → 8 sec on 31M rows.
examples.real_company: Unnamed education sector firm (per CelerData case study)

examples.description: Phase 1 (expand): Add new contact_method column, keep email. Phase 2 (migrate): Update application to write to contact_method, read from both. Phase 3 (contract): Drop email column. Zero downtime; clients at different versions work simultaneously.
examples.real_company: Any continuous deployment platform (Heroku, Stripe, Segment)
examples.metric: Zero production downtime; safe cross-version deployment

examples.scenario: PostgreSQL online schema migration: ADD COLUMN with DEFAULT on 500M-row table
examples.description: Without zero-downtime strategy, ALTER TABLE would lock table for 2+ hours. With expand-contract: (1) Add column, (2) backfill in batches, (3) update DEFAULT. pgroll automates with zero locking.
examples.real_company: Shopify, Slack, Discord (any platform with 500M+ row tables)
examples.metric: 2-3 hours of downtime avoided per migration

examples.scenario: Bounded context: 'User' in billing vs. authentication service
examples.description: Billing context cares about User.subscription_status, payment_method. Auth context cares about User.password_hash, mfa_enabled. Different domain models, separate tables, joined only at API boundary. Schema mutations in one context don't break the other.
examples.metric: Independent schema evolution per service; zero coordination overhead

analogies.analogy: Like a Git commit history: each version of a record is a separate 'commit' with timestamps. You never overwrite history; you append new versions. Point-in-time queries reconstruct the state at any past date.

Surrogate key = Social Security Number (immutable, never changes). Natural key = name + address (business meaning, but can change). If someone legally changes their name, Social Security Number is still valid; name-based keys break all references.

Like a dating app match table: (user_id, matched_user_id) records which users matched with whom. Fast lookup of 'who did user 123 match with?' via index on user_id. Without bridge table, storing all matches in a single array column forces sequential scan.

Renovating a house while living in it: (1) expand = add new room while living in old one, (2) migrate = move furniture and people to new room, (3) contract = tear down old room. Each step is reversible; you're never left homeless.

analogies.concept: Cardinality explosion in naive M:N design
analogies.analogy: Inviting 100 friends to a party and having 50 activities. Without a 'who-did-what' list (bridge table), you store attendance as: Friend.activities = [activity1, activity2, ...]. Filtering 'who did hiking?' forces checking all 100 friends' arrays. With bridge table, index (friend_id, activity_id) gives answer in microseconds.
Like keeping both a phone directory (normalized) and a Rolodex with extra notes (denormalized). Directory is space-efficient; Rolodex is query-efficient. Data warehouses use Rolodex logic.
Removing a player from a sports league's database, which automatically deletes all their game records. One DELETE command silently wipes out months of history. Dangerous unless carefully guarded.

Like how 'foot' means different things in podiatry (medical organ), soccer (body part used to kick), and UK slang (12 inches). Each domain has its own 'Customer' with different attributes and business rules. Forcing one definition breaks all three.

key_numbers.context: Education platform dashboard on 31M-row dataset; aggregate tables vs. normalized joins

key_numbers.metric: Index selectivity threshold for B-tree efficiency
key_numbers.context: Below 50% unique values in column, sequential scan faster than index lookup on typical hardware
Wrong join order selection or GROUP BY strategy due to histogram misestimate

key_numbers.metric: Star schema vs. normalized schema speedup
key_numbers.context: Fact table with dimension foreign keys vs. multi-table normalized schema with joins
Pre-aggregated fact tables at day/week/month level vs. granular scan + GROUP BY
Stripe Standard Data Warehouse; initial load available within 12 hours

key_numbers.metric: Downtime avoided with online schema migration
key_numbers.context: ALTER TABLE on 500M+ rows without expand-contract pattern

key_numbers.value: 30-50% less disk space for low-cardinality junction tables
key_numbers.context: Single foreign key column vs. multiple denormalized columns

common_misconceptions.misconception: Natural keys are better because they have business meaning.
common_misconceptions.reality: Natural keys couple schema to business logic. If the business attribute changes (email address, employee ID format), cascading updates ripple through all fact tables. Surrogate keys decouple analytics from operational mutations.

common_misconceptions.misconception: SCD Type 2 requires rewriting the entire dimension table.
common_misconceptions.reality: SCD Type 2 appends new rows with is_current flags. Zero rewrites. Queries efficiently filter on is_current=true for current state or join on effective_date range for historical state.

Denormalization for read-heavy OLAP workloads is standard practice. Write conflicts are avoided by refreshing aggregate tables in batch (nightly ETL), not real-time. Integrity is maintained by single source of truth in normalized operational schema.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Foreign key CASCADE options are always safe to use.
common_misconceptions.reality: ON DELETE CASCADE silently removes all matching rows in child tables. One DELETE statement can wipe millions of fact table rows without explicit visibility. Requires careful guard rails and backup strategies.
Expand-contract pattern (and tools like pgroll) enable zero-downtime migrations by running multiple schema versions in parallel. Requires three deployment phases but eliminates downtime entirely.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Low-cardinality columns should always have B-tree indexes.
common_misconceptions.reality: Low-cardinality columns (Booleans, flags, status enums) generate many row pointers per key value. Sequential table scan is faster than index lookup. Cardinality > 50% selectivity is typically needed for B-tree efficiency.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All entities should have a single definition across the system.
common_misconceptions.reality: Bounded contexts in DDD explicitly model the same real-world entity differently depending on domain language. A 'User' in billing, auth, and shipping domains have different attributes and behaviors. One definition breaks all three.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Bridging M:N relationships is optional for small datasets.
common_misconceptions.reality: Unnormalized M:N (storing arrays/strings in single columns) forces full-table scans on any filter. Bridge tables with proper indexes (even on 1K rows) scale horizontally without rework.
